title: Modernising the census: the process for change | Stats NZ
content:
## Modernising the census: the process for change
Information about why the census is changing, and the decision-making process.
Modernising the census describes how the census is changing.

## Why modernise the census
The practice of conducting censuses has been in existence for thousands of years. From around 2,500 BC, the Egyptians used censuses to work out the scale of the labour force they would need to build their pyramids.
In Aotearoa New Zealand, our first official population count was in 1851, and the national census evolved from there. Over the years, the census has been adapted to reflect our society, capturing the needs of New Zealanders and issues of the day.
Across the world, the way people use data is changing more than ever before. People want data that is more detailed and relevant to their daily lives – and they want it faster. Collecting and producing census data and statistics every five years is no longer fit-for-purpose.
As the country’s national statistics agency, we have recognised the need to do things differently and have been exploring how to modernise the census for a long time.

#### Quality data is vital for planning and decision-making today and for generations to come
So, at Stats NZ, we have been considering how we can:
Modernise the way we collect census information and make it easier for everyone to take part
Use existing, new and emerging data sources to produce quality data
Provide more relevant and timely information
We want to produce data and statistics that deliver the most value for the people, businesses and organisations of Aotearoa New Zealand. We want to make sure we’re responding to the needs of Aotearoa New Zealand today – and in the future – and do so in a way that is efficient, cost-effective, and sustainable.
To do this, we are adopting a new approach to the census that means we can produce and share more relevant, up-to-date data and statistics. And we will do it in ways that make it easier for us all.

## The process for change
While we’ve been considering how to modernise the census for over a decade, in 2024, we took some significant steps toward introducing a new approach.
We shared potential options for the future of the census with key partners and stakeholders (including iwi and Māori, community groups, government agencies, and researchers) to understand their data needs and perspectives.
We also asked the public for their feedback through a consultation process , to ensure a broad range of insights informed our work. We received 467 submissions (from 113 groups or organisations and 354 individuals).
In August 2024, we received expert advice from the Future Census Independent Evaluation Panel about the methodology and strategy we could use.
In September 2024, we published a feasibility report , outlining our technical readiness to make the shift towards an admin data-first census.
In October 2024, after careful consideration of all the feedback, analysis and information available, the Government Statistician made an in-principle decision about the future of the census in Aotearoa New Zealand and how the next census will run (also referred to as ‘the manner of taking’).
The Government Statistician’s assessment considered:
- the data needs of our customers and stakeholders
- data quality, flexibility, and timeliness
- Māori data needs and governance
- stakeholder and public sentiment
- feasibility, including cost and design complexity
- opportunities and challenges
- the impact of change on our data users and suppliers, and the wider data system.
The in-principle decision has now been endorsed by the Government and is being introduced in time for our next census.

### Cabinet papers and key documents
Cabinet papers and key documents shares more information on the government’s decision-making process to modernise the census since 2012, and other information that has supported and informed our work, including decision papers, reports, and notable research and methodology papers.
